lines: I am made of that self mettle as my sister
And prize me at her worth. In my true heart
I find she names my very deed of love;
Only she comes too short, that I profess
Myself an enemy to all other joys
Which the most precious square of sense
possesses,
And find I am alone felicitate
In your dear Highness' love.
fullText: I am made of that self mettle as my sister And prize me at her worth. In my true heart I find she names my very deed of love; Only she comes too short, that I profess Myself an enemy to all other joys Which the most precious square of sense possesses, And find I am alone felicitate In your dear Highness' love.

lines: Prescribe not us our duty.
fullText: Prescribe not us our duty.

lines: That's most certain, and with you; next month
with us.
fullText: That's most certain, and with you; next month with us.

lines: 'Tis the infirmity of his age. Yet he hath ever
but slenderly known himself.
fullText: 'Tis the infirmity of his age. Yet he hath ever but slenderly known himself.

lines: Such unconstant starts are we like to have
from him as this of Kent's banishment.
fullText: Such unconstant starts are we like to have from him as this of Kent's banishment.

lines: We shall further think of it.
fullText: We shall further think of it.

lines: If it be true, all vengeance comes too short
Which can pursue th' offender. How dost, my
lord?
fullText: If it be true, all vengeance comes too short Which can pursue th' offender. How dost, my lord?

lines: What, did my father's godson seek your life?
He whom my father named, your Edgar?
fullText: What, did my father's godson seek your life? He whom my father named, your Edgar?

lines: Was he not companion with the riotous knights
That tended upon my father?
fullText: Was he not companion with the riotous knights That tended upon my father?

lines: No marvel, then, though he were ill affected.
'Tis they have put him on the old man's death,
To have th' expense and waste of his revenues.
I have this present evening from my sister
Been well informed of them, and with such cautions
That if they come to sojourn at my house
I'll not be there.
fullText: No marvel, then, though he were ill affected. 'Tis they have put him on the old man's death, To have th' expense and waste of his revenues. I have this present evening from my sister Been well informed of them, and with such cautions That if they come to sojourn at my house I'll not be there.

lines: Thus out of season, threading dark-eyed night.
Occasions, noble Gloucester, of some poise,
Wherein we must have use of your advice.
Our father he hath writ, so hath our sister,
Of differences, which I best thought it fit
To answer from our home. The several messengers
From hence attend dispatch. Our good old friend,
Lay comforts to your bosom and bestow
Your needful counsel to our businesses,
Which craves the instant use.
fullText: Thus out of season, threading dark-eyed night. Occasions, noble Gloucester, of some poise, Wherein we must have use of your advice. Our father he hath writ, so hath our sister, Of differences, which I best thought it fit To answer from our home. The several messengers From hence attend dispatch. Our good old friend, Lay comforts to your bosom and bestow Your needful counsel to our businesses, Which craves the instant use.

lines: The messengers from our sister and the King.
fullText: The messengers from our sister and the King.

lines: Till noon? Till night, my lord, and all night, too.
fullText: Till noon? Till night, my lord, and all night, too.

lines: Sir, being his knave, I will.
fullText: Sir, being his knave, I will.

lines: My sister may receive it much more worse
To have her gentleman abused, assaulted
For following her affairs.--Put in his legs.
fullText: My sister may receive it much more worse To have her gentleman abused, assaulted For following her affairs.--Put in his legs.

lines: I am glad to see your Highness.
fullText: I am glad to see your Highness.

lines: I pray you, sir, take patience. I have hope
You less know how to value her desert
Than she to scant her duty.
fullText: I pray you, sir, take patience. I have hope You less know how to value her desert Than she to scant her duty.

lines: I cannot think my sister in the least
Would fail her obligation. If, sir, perchance
She have restrained the riots of your followers,
'Tis on such ground and to such wholesome end
As clears her from all blame.
fullText: I cannot think my sister in the least Would fail her obligation. If, sir, perchance She have restrained the riots of your followers, 'Tis on such ground and to such wholesome end As clears her from all blame.

lines: O sir, you are old.
Nature in you stands on the very verge
Of his confine. You should be ruled and led
By some discretion that discerns your state
Better than you yourself. Therefore, I pray you
That to our sister you do make return.
Say you have wronged her.
fullText: O sir, you are old. Nature in you stands on the very verge Of his confine. You should be ruled and led By some discretion that discerns your state Better than you yourself. Therefore, I pray you That to our sister you do make return. Say you have wronged her.

lines: Good sir, no more. These are unsightly tricks.
Return you to my sister.
fullText: Good sir, no more. These are unsightly tricks. Return you to my sister.

lines: O, the blest gods! So will you wish on me
When the rash mood is on.
fullText: O, the blest gods! So will you wish on me When the rash mood is on.

lines: Good sir, to th' purpose.
fullText: Good sir, to th' purpose.

lines: I know 't--my sister's. This approves her letter,
That she would soon be here.
fullText: I know 't--my sister's. This approves her letter, That she would soon be here.

lines: I pray you, father, being weak, seem so.
If till the expiration of your month
You will return and sojourn with my sister,
Dismissing half your train, come then to me.
I am now from home and out of that provision
Which shall be needful for your entertainment.
fullText: I pray you, father, being weak, seem so. If till the expiration of your month You will return and sojourn with my sister, Dismissing half your train, come then to me. I am now from home and out of that provision Which shall be needful for your entertainment.

lines: Not altogether so.
I looked not for you yet, nor am provided
For your fit welcome. Give ear, sir, to my sister,
For those that mingle reason with your passion
Must be content to think you old, and so--
But she knows what she does.
fullText: Not altogether so. I looked not for you yet, nor am provided For your fit welcome. Give ear, sir, to my sister, For those that mingle reason with your passion Must be content to think you old, and so-- But she knows what she does.

lines: I dare avouch it, sir. What, fifty followers?
Is it not well? What should you need of more?
Yea, or so many, sith that both charge and danger
Speak 'gainst so great a number? How in one house
Should many people under two commands
Hold amity? 'Tis hard, almost impossible.
fullText: I dare avouch it, sir. What, fifty followers? Is it not well? What should you need of more? Yea, or so many, sith that both charge and danger Speak 'gainst so great a number? How in one house Should many people under two commands Hold amity? 'Tis hard, almost impossible.

lines: Why not, my lord? If then they chanced to slack
you,
We could control them. If you will come to me
(For now I spy a danger), I entreat you
To bring but five-and-twenty. To no more
Will I give place or notice.
fullText: Why not, my lord? If then they chanced to slack you, We could control them. If you will come to me (For now I spy a danger), I entreat you To bring but five-and-twenty. To no more Will I give place or notice.

lines: And in good time you gave it.
fullText: And in good time you gave it.

lines: And speak 't again, my lord. No more with me.
fullText: And speak 't again, my lord. No more with me.

lines: What need one?
fullText: What need one?

lines: This house is little. The old man and 's people
Cannot be well bestowed.
fullText: This house is little. The old man and 's people Cannot be well bestowed.

lines: For his particular, I'll receive him gladly,
But not one follower.
fullText: For his particular, I'll receive him gladly, But not one follower.

lines: O sir, to willful men
The injuries that they themselves procure
Must be their schoolmasters. Shut up your doors.
He is attended with a desperate train,
And what they may incense him to, being apt
To have his ear abused, wisdom bids fear.
fullText: O sir, to willful men The injuries that they themselves procure Must be their schoolmasters. Shut up your doors. He is attended with a desperate train, And what they may incense him to, being apt To have his ear abused, wisdom bids fear.

lines: Hang him instantly.
fullText: Hang him instantly.

lines: Ingrateful fox! 'Tis he.
fullText: Ingrateful fox! 'Tis he.

lines: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!
fullText: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!

lines: So white, and such a traitor?
fullText: So white, and such a traitor?

lines: Be simple-answered, for we know the truth.
fullText: Be simple-answered, for we know the truth.

lines: To whose hands
You have sent the lunatic king. Speak.
fullText: To whose hands You have sent the lunatic king. Speak.

lines: And false.
fullText: And false.

lines: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charged at
peril--
fullText: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charged at peril--

lines: Wherefore to Dover?
fullText: Wherefore to Dover?

lines: One side will mock another. Th' other too.
fullText: One side will mock another. Th' other too.

lines: How now, you dog?
fullText: How now, you dog?

lines: Give me thy sword. A peasant stand up thus?
at him behind; kills him.]
fullText: Give me thy sword. A peasant stand up thus? at him behind; kills him.]

lines: Out, treacherous villain!
Thou call'st on him that hates thee. It was he
That made the overture of thy treasons to us,
Who is too good to pity thee.
fullText: Out, treacherous villain! Thou call'st on him that hates thee. It was he That made the overture of thy treasons to us, Who is too good to pity thee.

lines: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell
His way to Dover.
How is 't, my lord? How look you?
fullText: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell His way to Dover. How is 't, my lord? How look you?

lines: But are my brother's powers set forth?
fullText: But are my brother's powers set forth?

lines: Himself in person there?
fullText: Himself in person there?

lines: Lord Edmund spake not with your lord at home?
fullText: Lord Edmund spake not with your lord at home?

lines: What might import my sister's letter to him?
fullText: What might import my sister's letter to him?

lines: Faith, he is posted hence on serious matter.
It was great ignorance, Gloucester's eyes being out,
To let him live. Where he arrives he moves
All hearts against us. Edmund, I think, is gone,
In pity of his misery, to dispatch
His nighted life; moreover to descry
The strength o' th' enemy.
fullText: Faith, he is posted hence on serious matter. It was great ignorance, Gloucester's eyes being out, To let him live. Where he arrives he moves All hearts against us. Edmund, I think, is gone, In pity of his misery, to dispatch His nighted life; moreover to descry The strength o' th' enemy.

lines: Our troops set forth tomorrow. Stay with us.
The ways are dangerous.
fullText: Our troops set forth tomorrow. Stay with us. The ways are dangerous.

lines: Why should she write to Edmund? Might not you
Transport her purposes by word? Belike,
Some things--I know not what. I'll love thee much--
Let me unseal the letter.
fullText: Why should she write to Edmund? Might not you Transport her purposes by word? Belike, Some things--I know not what. I'll love thee much-- Let me unseal the letter.

lines: I know your lady does not love her husband;
I am sure of that; and at her late being here,
She gave strange eliads and most speaking looks
To noble Edmund. I know you are of her bosom.
fullText: I know your lady does not love her husband; I am sure of that; and at her late being here, She gave strange eliads and most speaking looks To noble Edmund. I know you are of her bosom.

lines: I speak in understanding. Y' are; I know 't.
Therefore I do advise you take this note:
My lord is dead; Edmund and I have talked,
And more convenient is he for my hand
Than for your lady's. You may gather more.
If you do find him, pray you, give him this,
And when your mistress hears thus much from you,
I pray, desire her call her wisdom to her.
So, fare you well.
If you do chance to hear of that blind traitor,
Preferment falls on him that cuts him off.
fullText: I speak in understanding. Y' are; I know 't. Therefore I do advise you take this note: My lord is dead; Edmund and I have talked, And more convenient is he for my hand Than for your lady's. You may gather more. If you do find him, pray you, give him this, And when your mistress hears thus much from you, I pray, desire her call her wisdom to her. So, fare you well. If you do chance to hear of that blind traitor, Preferment falls on him that cuts him off.

lines: Fare thee well.
fullText: Fare thee well.

lines: Our sister's man is certainly miscarried.
fullText: Our sister's man is certainly miscarried.

lines: Now, sweet lord,
You know the goodness I intend upon you;
Tell me but truly, but then speak the truth,
Do you not love my sister?
fullText: Now, sweet lord, You know the goodness I intend upon you; Tell me but truly, but then speak the truth, Do you not love my sister?

lines: But have you never found my brother's way
To the forfended place?
fullText: But have you never found my brother's way To the forfended place?

lines: I am doubtful that you have been conjunct
And bosomed with her as far as we call hers.
fullText: I am doubtful that you have been conjunct And bosomed with her as far as we call hers.

lines: I never shall endure her. Dear my lord,
Be not familiar with her.
fullText: I never shall endure her. Dear my lord, Be not familiar with her.

lines: Why is this reasoned?
fullText: Why is this reasoned?

lines: Sister, you'll go with us?
fullText: Sister, you'll go with us?

lines: 'Tis most convenient. Pray, go with us.
fullText: 'Tis most convenient. Pray, go with us.

lines: That's as we list to grace him.
Methinks our pleasure might have been demanded
Ere you had spoke so far. He led our powers,
Bore the commission of my place and person,
The which immediacy may well stand up
And call itself your brother.
fullText: That's as we list to grace him. Methinks our pleasure might have been demanded Ere you had spoke so far. He led our powers, Bore the commission of my place and person, The which immediacy may well stand up And call itself your brother.

lines: In my rights,
By me invested, he compeers the best.
fullText: In my rights, By me invested, he compeers the best.

lines: Jesters do oft prove prophets.
fullText: Jesters do oft prove prophets.

lines: Lady, I am not well, else I should answer
From a full-flowing stomach.
General,
Take thou my soldiers, prisoners, patrimony.
Dispose of them, of me; the walls is thine.
Witness the world that I create thee here
My lord and master.
fullText: Lady, I am not well, else I should answer From a full-flowing stomach. General, Take thou my soldiers, prisoners, patrimony. Dispose of them, of me; the walls is thine. Witness the world that I create thee here My lord and master.

lines: Let the drum strike, and prove my title thine.
fullText: Let the drum strike, and prove my title thine.

lines: Sick, O, sick!
fullText: Sick, O, sick!

lines: My sickness grows upon me.
fullText: My sickness grows upon me.
